claims.label: confirmed

missed_changes: Onchain financial/transactional privacy emerged as a major, explicitly emphasized excitement area distinct from proof-of-personhood — endorsing Railgun, Aleo, Aztec, Inco, Seismic, TACEO and calling lack of onchain privacy 'one of the biggest sources of friction, if not a dealbreaker, for institutions… critical.'

Strong new enthusiasm for 'arcade tokens'/apptokens as 'the most underappreciated token type in crypto' for app/game monetization — a positively-viewed category beyond the network-token-vs-memecoin framing.
New push on legal/organizational structures for decentralization: declared 'the end of the foundation model' and promoted DUNAs/BORGs.
New focus on airdrops and incentive/distribution design, plus AI-driven sybil/airdrop-farming as an escalating threat (multi-episode treatment).
New concrete concern that non-deterministic bridge outputs break cross-chain payments — a specific blocker raised repeatedly despite overall payments optimism.
New concern about consumer platform/OS openness regressions (app stores replacing direct downloads, 'liquid glass') as gatekeeping, extending the open-network thesis to PCs.
notes: As a builder, Eddy stayed highly consistent: every excited sector except fully-on-chain-games and every concern except BTC's security budget was reaffirmed, often heavily (Jolt/ZK, proof-of-human/World, scaling/blockspace, decentralized social, payments, memecoin/company-token critiques, ETH & UNI as network tokens, fundamentals-over-sentiment). No contradictions. The two 'absent' items: fully-on-chain games as a thesis was never revisited (the arcade-token/game-monetization talk is about token design, not autonomous worlds), and his BTC comments in-window ('bitcoin envy,' anti-DCF-via-BTC) never touch the long-term security-budget worry. The biggest unanticipated development is onchain financial privacy moving to center stage.
